␀ ␀ ␀a␀r␀e␀ ␀y␀o␀u␀ ␀c␀o␀n␀f␀u␀s␀e␀d␀ ␀b␀y␀ ␀t␀h␀i␀s␀ ␀y␀e␀t␀ ? ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀f␀i␀n␀a␀l␀l␀y␀ , ␀ ␀a␀l␀l␀ ␀t␀h␀e␀ ␀e␀x␀p␀e␀r␀i␀e␀n␀c␀e␀ ␀h␀o␀u␀r␀s␀ ␀a␀n␀d␀ ␀s␀u␀p␀e␀r␀v␀i␀s␀i␀o␀n␀ ␀m␀u␀s␀t␀ ␀b␀e␀ ␀c␀i␀r␀c␀u␀m␀s␀t␀a␀n␀c␀e␀s␀ ␀a␀n␀d␀ ␀t␀h␀e␀ ␀t␀r␀i␀c␀k␀ ␀i␀n␀ ␀g␀e␀t␀t␀i␀n␀g␀ ␀t␀h␀e␀ ␀r␀i␀g␀h␀t␀ ␀o␀n␀e␀ ␀i␀s␀ ␀d␀e␀t␀e␀r␀m␀i␀n␀i␀n␀g␀ ␀t␀h␀e␀ ␀b␀e␀s␀t␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀a␀n␀s␀w␀e␀r␀ ␀o␀r␀ ␀t␀h␀e␀ ␀o␀n␀e␀ ␀t␀h␀a␀t␀ ␀i␀s␀ ␀m␀o␀s␀t␀ ␀c␀o␀r␀r␀e␀c␀t␀ ! ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀s␀o␀ ␀a␀f␀t␀e␀r␀ ␀t␀h␀e␀ ␀f␀i␀r␀s␀t␀ ␀p␀a␀r␀t␀ ␀i␀s␀ ␀p␀a␀s␀s␀e␀d␀ , ␀ ␀t␀h␀e␀n␀ ␀a␀n␀ ␀m␀f␀t␀ ␀i␀n␀t␀e␀r␀n␀ ␀c␀a␀n␀ ␀t␀a␀k␀e␀ ␀t␀h␀e␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀n␀o␀w␀ ␀i␀t␀ ␀'␀s␀ ␀w␀r␀i␀t␀t␀e␀n␀ ␀a␀n␀d␀ ␀i␀ ␀t␀h␀i␀n␀k␀ ␀t␀h␀e␀ ␀t␀e␀s␀t␀ ␀i␀s␀ ␀o␀n␀e␀ ␀w␀h␀e␀r␀e␀ ␀t␀h␀e␀r␀e␀ ␀a␀r␀e␀ ␀s␀e␀v␀e␀r␀a␀l␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀c␀l␀i␀n␀i␀c␀a␀l␀ ␀v␀i␀g␀n␀e␀t␀t␀e␀s␀ ␀a␀n␀d␀ ␀s␀i␀t␀u␀a␀t␀i␀o␀n␀s␀ ␀p␀r␀e␀s␀e␀n␀t␀e␀d␀ ␀a␀n␀d␀ ␀t␀h␀e␀ ␀m␀f␀t␀ ␀i␀n␀t␀e␀r␀n␀ ␀e␀x␀p␀l␀a␀i␀n␀s␀ ␀a␀l␀l␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀t␀h␀e␀ ␀a␀s␀p␀e␀c␀t␀s␀ ␀a␀n␀d␀ ␀c␀l␀i␀n␀i␀c␀a␀l␀ , ␀ ␀t␀h␀e␀o␀r␀e␀t␀i␀c␀a␀l␀ , ␀ ␀l␀e␀g␀a␀l␀ ␀a␀n␀d␀ ␀e␀t␀h␀i␀c␀a␀l␀ ␀r␀a␀t␀i␀o␀n␀a␀l␀e␀ ␀f␀o␀r␀ ␀t␀h␀e␀i␀r␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀c␀h␀o␀i␀c␀e␀ ␀o␀f␀ ␀c␀o␀u␀r␀s␀e␀ ␀o␀f␀ ␀a␀c␀t␀i␀o␀n␀.␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀i␀ ␀w␀o␀u␀l␀d␀ ␀m␀u␀c␀h␀ ␀r␀a␀t␀h␀e␀r␀ ␀d␀o␀ ␀t␀h␀i␀s␀ ␀i␀n␀ ␀e␀s␀s␀a␀y␀ ␀o␀r␀ ␀w␀r␀i␀t␀t␀e␀n␀ ␀f␀o␀r␀m␀ ␀t␀h␀a␀n␀ ␀b␀e␀ ␀i␀n␀ ␀a␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀r␀o␀o␀m␀ ␀w␀i␀t␀h␀ ␀a␀ ␀p␀a␀n␀e␀l␀ ␀o␀f␀ ␀i␀n␀t␀e␀r␀v␀i␀e␀w␀e␀r␀s␀ ␀t␀o␀ ␀w␀h␀o␀m␀ ␀i␀ ␀w␀o␀u␀l␀d␀ ␀h␀a␀v␀e␀ ␀t␀o␀ ␀e␀x␀p␀l␀a␀i␀n␀ ␀m␀y␀s␀e␀l␀f␀ ! ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀i␀ ␀t␀h␀i␀n␀k␀ ␀i␀t␀ ␀m␀a␀y␀ ␀n␀o␀w␀ ␀b␀e␀ ␀a␀u␀t␀o␀m␀a␀t␀e␀d␀ ␀b␀y␀ ␀c␀o␀m␀p␀u␀t␀e␀r␀ , ␀ ␀l␀i␀k␀e␀ ␀t␀h␀e␀ ␀f␀i␀r␀s␀t␀ ␀p␀a␀r␀t␀ ␀o␀f␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀t␀h␀e␀ ␀e␀x␀a␀m␀ ␀i␀s␀ ␀w␀i␀t␀h␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀m␀u␀l␀t␀i␀p␀l␀e␀ ␀c␀h␀o␀i␀c␀e␀ ␀q␀u␀e␀s␀t␀i␀o␀n␀s␀.␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀w␀e␀l␀l␀ , ␀ ␀a␀s␀ ␀t␀h␀i␀n␀g␀s␀ ␀a␀r␀e␀ ␀n␀o␀w␀ , ␀ ␀i␀ ␀h␀a␀v␀e␀ ␀a␀c␀c␀u␀m␀u␀l␀a␀t␀e␀d␀ ␀a␀l␀l␀ ␀t␀h␀e␀ ␀r␀e␀q␀u␀i␀r␀e␀d␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀h␀o␀u␀r␀s␀ ␀f␀o␀r␀ ␀e␀v␀e␀r␀y␀ ␀c␀a␀t␀e␀g␀o␀r␀y␀ ␀e␀x␀c␀e␀p␀t␀ ␀t␀h␀e␀ ␀a␀r␀e␀a␀ ␀o␀f␀ ␀c␀h␀i␀l␀d␀ , ␀ ␀f␀a␀m␀i␀l␀y␀ ␀o␀r␀ ␀c␀o␀u␀p␀l␀e␀s␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀c␀o␀u␀n␀s␀e␀l␀i␀n␀g␀ ␀ & ␀ ␀p␀s␀y␀c␀h␀o␀t␀h␀e␀r␀a␀p␀y␀.␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀i␀n␀ ␀t␀h␀a␀t␀ ␀c␀a␀t␀e␀g␀o␀r␀y␀ ␀i␀ ␀n␀o␀w␀ ␀h␀a␀v␀e␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀m␀o␀r␀e␀ ␀t␀o␀ ␀g␀e␀t␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ( ␀ ␀m␀i␀n␀ ) ␀ , ␀ ␀b␀u␀t␀ ␀w␀h␀e␀n␀ ␀i␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀a␀d␀d␀ ␀a␀l␀l␀ ␀t␀h␀e␀ ␀h␀o␀u␀r␀s␀ ␀i␀n␀ ␀a␀l␀l␀ ␀t␀h␀e␀ ␀c␀a␀t␀e␀g␀o␀r␀i␀e␀s␀ , ␀ ␀i␀ ␀f␀i␀n␀d␀ ␀a␀ ␀d␀e␀f␀i␀c␀i␀t␀ ␀o␀f␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀m␀o␀r␀e␀.␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀a␀l␀t␀h␀o␀u␀g␀h␀ ␀t␀h␀i␀s␀ ␀m␀a␀y␀ ␀s␀o␀u␀n␀d␀ ␀c␀o␀n␀f␀u␀s␀i␀n␀g␀ ␀l␀e␀t␀ ␀m␀e␀ ␀e␀x␀p␀l␀a␀i␀n␀ ; ␀ ␀o␀v␀e␀r␀a␀l␀l␀ ␀i␀'␀m␀ ␀n␀o␀w␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀h␀o␀u␀r␀s␀ ␀s␀ ␀'␀m␀r␀t␀ ␀o␀f␀ ␀t␀h␀e␀ ␀r␀e␀q␀u␀i␀r␀e␀d␀ ␀ ␀ , ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀t␀o␀t␀a␀l␀ , ␀ ␀a␀n␀d␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀h␀o␀u␀r␀s␀ ␀s␀h␀o␀r␀t␀ ␀o␀f␀ ␀t␀h␀e␀ ␀r␀e␀q␀u␀i␀r␀e␀d␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀m␀i␀n␀i␀m␀u␀m␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀h␀o␀u␀r␀s␀ ␀f␀o␀r␀ ␀w␀o␀r␀k␀ ␀w␀i␀t␀h␀ ␀c␀h␀i␀l␀d␀r␀e␀n␀ , ␀ ␀f␀a␀m␀i␀l␀i␀e␀s␀ ␀o␀r␀ ␀c␀o␀u␀p␀l␀e␀s␀.␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀i␀n␀ ␀o␀t␀h␀e␀r␀ ␀w␀o␀r␀d␀s␀ , ␀ ␀i␀'␀v␀e␀ ␀a␀c␀c␀u␀m␀u␀l␀a␀t␀e␀ ␀'␀v␀e␀o␀ ␀f␀a␀r␀ ␀ ␀ , ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀h␀o␀u␀r␀s␀.␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀m␀o␀r␀e␀ ␀m␀u␀s␀t␀ ␀b␀e␀ ␀w␀i␀t␀h␀ ␀c␀h␀i␀l␀d␀r␀e␀n␀ , ␀ ␀f␀a␀m␀i␀l␀i␀e␀s␀ ␀o␀r␀ ␀c␀o␀u␀p␀l␀e␀s␀.␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀t␀h␀e␀ ␀r␀e␀m␀a␀i␀n␀i␀n␀g␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀c␀a␀n␀ ␀a␀l␀s␀o␀ ␀b␀e␀ ␀w␀i␀t␀h␀ ␀c␀h␀i␀l␀d␀r␀e␀n␀ , ␀ ␀f␀a␀m␀i␀l␀i␀e␀s␀ ␀o␀r␀ ␀c␀o␀u␀p␀l␀e␀s␀ , ␀ ␀o␀r␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀w␀i␀t␀h␀ ␀o␀n␀e␀-␀t␀o␀-␀o␀n␀e␀ ␀t␀h␀e␀r␀a␀p␀y␀ ␀w␀i␀t␀h␀ ␀a␀d␀u␀l␀t␀s␀.␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀p␀l␀e␀a␀s␀e␀ ␀l␀e␀t␀ ␀m␀e␀ ␀k␀n␀o␀w␀ ␀w␀h␀e␀t␀h␀e␀r␀ ␀i␀'␀v␀e␀ ␀t␀h␀o␀r␀o␀u␀g␀h␀l␀y␀ ␀'␀v␀e␀n␀f␀u␀s␀e␀d␀ ␀y␀o␀u␀ ␀o␀r␀ ␀w␀h␀e␀t␀h␀e␀r␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀a␀l␀l␀ ␀t␀h␀i␀s␀ ␀m␀a␀k␀e␀s␀ ␀s␀e␀n␀s␀e␀ ! ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀s␀o␀ ␀n␀o␀w␀ ␀i␀'␀m␀ ␀w␀o␀r␀k␀i␀n␀g␀ ␀w␀i␀t␀h␀ ␀c␀h␀i␀ ␀'␀m␀r␀e␀n␀ ␀a␀t␀ ␀t␀h␀e␀ ␀f␀a␀m␀i␀l␀y␀ ␀s␀e␀r␀v␀i␀c␀e␀ ␀a␀g␀e␀n␀c␀y␀ ␀o␀f␀ ␀s␀a␀n␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀m␀a␀t␀e␀o␀ ␀c␀o␀u␀n␀t␀y␀ , ␀ ␀i␀n␀ ␀t␀h␀e␀i␀r␀ ␀c␀h␀i␀l␀d␀ ␀a␀b␀u␀s␀e␀ ␀t␀r␀e␀a␀t␀m␀e␀n␀t␀ ␀o␀r␀ ␀ '' ␀ ␀c␀h␀a␀t␀ '' ␀ ␀p␀r␀o␀g␀r␀a␀m␀.␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀i␀ ␀s␀e␀e␀ ␀a␀b␀o␀u␀t␀ ␀ ␀ ␀o␀r␀ ␀ ␀ ␀k␀i␀d␀s␀ ␀e␀a␀c␀h␀ ␀w␀e␀e␀k␀ ␀s␀o␀ ␀i␀t␀ ␀c␀o␀u␀l␀d␀ ␀t␀a␀k␀e␀ ␀a␀n␀o␀t␀h␀e␀r␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀w␀e␀e␀k␀s␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀a␀t␀ ␀t␀h␀i␀s␀ ␀r␀a␀t␀e␀ ␀t␀o␀ ␀g␀e␀t␀ ␀t␀h␀e␀ ␀r␀e␀m␀a␀i␀n␀i␀n␀g␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀t␀h␀a␀t␀ ␀i␀ ␀n␀e␀e␀d␀ , ␀ ␀b␀u␀t␀ ␀t␀h␀e␀ ␀p␀r␀o␀g␀r␀a␀m␀ ␀'␀s␀ ␀g␀r␀a␀n␀t␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀e␀x␀p␀i␀r␀e␀s␀ ␀i␀n␀ ␀s␀e␀p␀t␀e␀m␀b␀e␀r␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀.␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀a␀n␀o␀t␀h␀e␀r␀ ␀g␀r␀a␀n␀t␀ ␀i␀s␀ ␀b␀e␀i␀n␀g␀ ␀s␀u␀b␀m␀i␀t␀t␀e␀d␀ ␀t␀o␀ ␀e␀x␀t␀e␀n␀d␀ ␀t␀h␀e␀ ␀p␀r␀o␀g␀r␀a␀m␀ , ␀ ␀t␀h␀o␀u␀g␀h␀ ␀i␀t␀ ␀'␀s␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀h␀a␀r␀d␀ ␀t␀o␀ ␀s␀a␀y␀ ␀n␀o␀w␀ ␀w␀h␀e␀t␀h␀e␀r␀ ␀i␀t␀ ␀w␀i␀l␀l␀ ␀b␀e␀ ␀a␀c␀c␀e␀p␀t␀e␀d␀ ␀ & ␀ ␀a␀p␀p␀r␀o␀v␀e␀d␀.␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀r␀e␀g␀a␀r␀d␀l␀e␀s␀s␀ , ␀ ␀i␀'␀m␀ ␀a␀l␀w␀a␀y␀s␀ ␀a␀s␀k␀i␀n␀g␀ ␀'␀m␀r␀ ␀m␀o␀r␀e␀ ␀c␀h␀i␀l␀d␀r␀e␀n␀ ␀c␀l␀i␀e␀n␀t␀s␀ ␀t␀o␀ ␀w␀o␀r␀k␀ ␀w␀i␀t␀h␀ , ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀a␀n␀d␀ ␀g␀e␀t␀t␀i␀n␀g␀ ␀s␀o␀m␀e␀ ␀e␀v␀e␀r␀y␀ ␀n␀o␀w␀ ␀ & ␀ ␀t␀h␀e␀n␀ ␀b␀u␀t␀ ␀t␀h␀e␀ ␀n␀e␀w␀ ␀c␀l␀i␀e␀n␀t␀s␀ ␀o␀n␀l␀y␀ ␀s␀e␀e␀m␀ ␀t␀o␀ ␀c␀o␀m␀e␀ ␀i␀n␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀a␀b␀o␀u␀t␀ ␀e␀q␀u␀a␀l␀ ␀t␀o␀ ␀t␀h␀e␀ ␀r␀a␀t␀e␀ ␀o␀f␀ ␀a␀t␀t␀r␀i␀t␀i␀o␀n␀.␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀y␀o␀u␀ ␀s␀e␀e␀ , ␀ ␀i␀n␀ ␀f␀a␀m␀i␀l␀y␀ ␀'␀s␀ ␀w␀i␀t␀h␀ ␀c␀u␀r␀r␀e␀n␀t␀ ␀o␀r␀ ␀h␀i␀s␀t␀o␀r␀i␀e␀s␀ ␀o␀f␀ ␀a␀b␀u␀s␀e␀ ␀h␀a␀v␀e␀ ␀a␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀h␀i␀g␀h␀ ␀l␀e␀v␀e␀l␀ ␀o␀f␀ ␀d␀y␀s␀f␀u␀n␀c␀t␀i␀o␀n␀ ␀a␀n␀d␀ ␀p␀r␀o␀b␀l␀e␀m␀s␀ ␀a␀r␀e␀ ␀e␀n␀c␀o␀u␀n␀t␀e␀r␀e␀d␀ ␀w␀i␀t␀h␀ ␀c␀o␀m␀m␀i␀t␀m␀e␀n␀t␀ ␀t␀o␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀k␀e␀e␀p␀ ␀r␀e␀g␀u␀l␀a␀r␀ ␀w␀e␀e␀k␀l␀y␀ ␀t␀h␀e␀r␀a␀p␀y␀ ␀a␀p␀p␀o␀i␀n␀t␀m␀e␀n␀t␀s␀ , ␀ ␀l␀e␀t␀ ␀a␀l␀o␀n␀e␀ ␀p␀r␀o␀b␀l␀e␀m␀s␀ ␀w␀i␀t␀h␀ ␀f␀a␀m␀i␀l␀y␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀f␀i␀n␀a␀n␀c␀e␀ ␀f␀o␀r␀ ␀k␀e␀e␀p␀i␀n␀g␀ ␀t␀h␀e␀ ␀y␀e␀a␀r␀ ! ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀a␀n␀d␀ ␀i␀f␀ ␀t␀h␀e␀ ␀f␀a␀m␀i␀l␀y␀ ␀s␀e␀r␀v␀i␀c␀e␀ ␀a␀g␀e␀n␀c␀y␀ ␀g␀r␀a␀n␀t␀ ␀i␀s␀ ␀a␀p␀p␀r␀o␀v␀e␀d␀ , ␀ ␀i␀ ␀c␀a␀n␀ ␀c␀o␀n␀t␀i␀n␀u␀e␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀w␀o␀r␀k␀i␀n␀g␀ ␀t␀h␀e␀r␀e␀ ␀c␀o␀n␀c␀u␀r␀r␀e␀n␀t␀l␀y␀ ! ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀i␀t␀ ␀f␀e␀e␀l␀s␀ ␀g␀r␀e␀a␀t␀ ␀t␀o␀ ␀k␀n␀o␀w␀ ␀t␀h␀i␀s␀ ␀p␀h␀a␀s␀e␀ ␀w␀i␀l␀l␀ ␀s␀o␀o␀n␀ ␀b␀e␀ ␀d␀o␀n␀e␀.␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀w␀i␀t␀h␀ ␀a␀l␀l␀ ␀t␀h␀a␀t␀ ␀s␀a␀i␀d␀ , ␀ ␀b␀e␀f␀o␀r␀e␀ ␀i␀ ␀s␀i␀g␀n␀ ␀o␀f␀f␀ ␀f␀o␀r␀ ␀n␀o␀w␀ , ␀ ␀m␀y␀ ␀s␀i␀s␀t␀e␀r␀-␀i␀n␀-␀l␀a␀w␀ , ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀m␀e␀l␀i␀s␀s␀a␀ ␀'␀s␀ ␀s␀i␀s␀t␀e␀r␀ ␀s␀u␀s␀a␀n␀n␀a␀h␀ ␀f␀r␀e␀n␀c␀h␀ , ␀ ␀h␀a␀s␀ ␀w␀r␀i␀t␀t␀e␀n␀ ␀a␀ ␀c␀h␀i␀l␀d␀r␀e␀n␀ ␀'␀s␀ ␀n␀o␀v␀e␀l␀ ␀e␀n␀t␀i␀t␀l␀e␀d␀ ␀ '' ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀o␀p␀e␀r␀a␀t␀i␀o␀n␀ ␀r␀e␀d␀w␀o␀o␀d␀ '' ␀ ␀u␀s␀i␀n␀g␀ ␀t␀h␀e␀ ␀p␀e␀n␀ ␀n␀a␀m␀e␀ ␀s␀.␀ ␀t␀e␀r␀r␀e␀l␀l␀ ␀f␀r␀e␀n␀c␀h␀ ␀a␀n␀d␀ ␀i␀t␀ ␀h␀a␀s␀ ␀j␀u␀s␀t␀ ␀b␀e␀e␀n␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀p␀u␀b␀l␀i␀s␀h␀e␀d␀ ␀a␀n␀d␀ ␀i␀s␀ ␀b␀e␀i␀n␀g␀ ␀d␀i␀s␀t␀r␀i␀b␀u␀t␀e␀d␀ ␀t␀o␀ ␀b␀o␀o␀k␀s␀t␀o␀r␀e␀s␀ ␀a␀n␀d␀ ␀a␀v␀a␀i␀l␀a␀b␀l␀e␀ ␀o␀n␀ ␀a␀m␀a␀z␀o␀n␀.␀c␀o␀m␀ ! ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀l␀o␀o␀k␀ ␀a␀t␀ ␀h␀e␀r␀ ␀w␀e␀b␀s␀i␀t␀e␀ : ␀ ␀w␀w␀w␀.␀o␀p␀e␀r␀a␀t␀i␀o␀n␀r␀e␀d␀w␀o␀o␀d␀.␀c␀o␀m␀ , ␀ ␀a␀n␀d␀ ␀i␀'␀m␀ ␀r␀e␀a␀d␀i␀n␀g␀ ␀i␀t␀ ␀'␀m␀ ␀ ␀a␀l␀o␀u␀d␀ ␀a␀t␀ ␀n␀i␀g␀h␀t␀ ␀a␀t␀ ␀b␀e␀d␀t␀i␀m␀e␀ ␀t␀o␀ ␀m␀e␀l␀i␀s␀s␀a␀.␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀a␀l␀s␀o␀ , ␀ ␀s␀h␀e␀ ␀r␀e␀a␀d␀ ␀y␀o␀u␀r␀ ␀l␀e␀t␀t␀e␀r␀ ␀a␀n␀d␀ ␀w␀a␀n␀t␀e␀d␀ ␀t␀o␀ ␀k␀n␀o␀w␀ ␀i␀f␀ ␀y␀o␀u␀ ␀w␀o␀u␀l␀d␀ ␀l␀i␀k␀e␀ ␀a␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀r␀e␀p␀l␀y␀ ␀f␀r␀o␀m␀ ␀h␀e␀r␀ ␀a␀s␀ ␀w␀e␀l␀l␀.␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀i␀ ␀h␀o␀p␀e␀ ␀t␀h␀i␀s␀ ␀r␀e␀p␀l␀y␀ ␀c␀o␀m␀e␀s␀ ␀i␀n␀ ␀t␀i␀m␀e␀ ␀t␀o␀ ␀b␀e␀ ␀h␀e␀l␀p␀f␀u␀l␀ ␀t␀o␀ ␀y␀o␀u␀r␀ ␀p␀r␀o␀j␀e␀c␀t␀ , ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀a␀l␀l␀ ␀t␀h␀e␀ ␀b␀e␀s␀t␀ ␀r␀e␀c␀e␀n␀t␀ ␀l␀e␀t␀t␀e␀r␀ ; ␀ ␀w␀e␀ ␀r␀e␀a␀d␀ ␀i␀t␀ ␀o␀v␀e␀r␀ ␀d␀i␀n␀n␀e␀r␀ ␀t␀o␀n␀i␀g␀h␀t␀.␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀s␀o␀u␀n␀d␀s␀ ␀l␀i␀k␀e␀ ␀y␀o␀u␀ ␀a␀r␀e␀ ␀s␀t␀a␀r␀t␀i␀n␀g␀ ␀a␀ ␀b␀i␀g␀ ␀a␀d␀v␀e␀n␀t␀u␀r␀e␀ ␀-␀ ␀-␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀s␀o␀m␀e␀ ␀i␀n␀t␀e␀r␀e␀s␀t␀i␀n␀g␀ ␀c␀o␀l␀l␀e␀a␀g␀u␀e␀s␀ ␀w␀i␀t␀h␀ ␀w␀h␀o␀m␀ ␀t␀o␀ ␀w␀o␀r␀k␀.␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀i␀ ␀d␀o␀n␀'␀t␀ ␀r␀e␀a␀l␀l␀y␀ ␀k␀n␀o␀w␀ ␀t␀h␀e␀ ␀a␀r␀e␀a␀ ␀w␀h␀e␀r␀e␀ ␀y␀o␀u␀ ␀a␀r␀e␀ ␀l␀i␀v␀i␀n␀g␀ , ␀ ␀b␀u␀t␀ ␀m␀y␀ ␀s␀i␀s␀t␀e␀r␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀l␀i␀v␀e␀d␀ ␀i␀n␀ ␀c␀a␀m␀b␀r␀i␀d␀g␀e␀ ␀w␀h␀e␀n␀ ␀s␀h␀e␀ ␀w␀a␀s␀ ␀a␀t␀ ␀h␀a␀r␀v␀a␀r␀d␀ ␀ & ␀ ␀i␀ ␀c␀a␀n␀ ␀t␀e␀l␀l␀ ␀y␀o␀u␀ ␀t␀h␀a␀t␀ ␀d␀o␀e␀s␀ ␀s␀e␀e␀m␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀l␀i␀k␀e␀ ␀a␀ ␀d␀y␀n␀a␀m␀i␀c␀ , ␀ ␀h␀a␀p␀p␀e␀n␀i␀n␀g␀ ␀p␀l␀a␀c␀e␀ ␀t␀o␀ ␀l␀i␀v␀e␀.␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀w␀h␀e␀n␀ ␀s␀h␀e␀ ␀w␀a␀s␀ ␀t␀h␀e␀r␀e␀ ␀t␀r␀a␀c␀e␀y␀ ␀c␀h␀a␀p␀m␀a␀n␀ ␀w␀a␀s␀ ␀a␀ ␀s␀t␀r␀e␀e␀t␀ ␀m␀u␀s␀i␀c␀i␀a␀n␀ ␀ & ␀ ␀i␀c␀e␀ ␀c␀r␀e␀a␀m␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀s␀t␀o␀r␀e␀s␀/␀s␀h␀o␀p␀s␀ ␀h␀a␀d␀ ␀t␀h␀i␀s␀ ␀t␀a␀s␀t␀y␀ ␀t␀r␀e␀a␀t␀ : ␀ ␀ '' ␀ ␀m␀i␀x␀ ␀i␀n␀s␀ '' ␀ ␀w␀h␀e␀r␀e␀ ␀t␀h␀e␀y␀ ␀w␀o␀u␀l␀d␀ ␀m␀i␀x␀ ␀i␀n␀ ␀j␀o␀b␀ ␀-␀ ␀-␀ ␀t␀h␀e␀ ␀p␀a␀t␀i␀e␀n␀t␀s␀ ␀a␀r␀e␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀g␀r␀e␀a␀t␀ , ␀ ␀i␀ ␀f␀e␀e␀l␀ ␀v␀e␀r␀y␀ ␀s␀u␀p␀p␀o␀r␀t␀e␀d␀ ␀i␀n␀ ␀m␀y␀ ␀w␀o␀r␀k␀ ␀ & ␀ ␀i␀ ␀l␀i␀k␀e␀ ␀t␀h␀e␀ ␀c␀o␀n␀t␀i␀n␀u␀i␀t␀y␀ ␀i␀n␀ ␀i␀n␀ ␀a␀s␀h␀l␀a␀n␀d␀ ␀w␀h␀e␀r␀e␀ ␀w␀e␀ ␀s␀t␀a␀y␀e␀d␀ ␀f␀o␀r␀ ␀ ␀ ␀n␀i␀g␀h␀t␀s␀ ␀i␀n␀ ␀a␀ ␀g␀r␀e␀a␀t␀ ␀y␀o␀u␀t␀h␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀h␀o␀s␀t␀e␀l␀ ␀v␀e␀r␀y␀ ␀n␀e␀a␀r␀ ␀t␀h␀e␀ ␀s␀h␀a␀k␀e␀s␀p␀e␀a␀r␀e␀ ␀f␀e␀s␀t␀i␀v␀a␀l␀.␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀w␀e␀ ␀s␀a␀w␀ ␀t␀h␀e␀ ␀w␀o␀r␀l␀d␀ ␀p␀r␀e␀m␀i␀e␀r␀ ␀o␀f␀ ␀a␀ ␀n␀e␀w␀ ␀p␀r␀o␀d␀u␀c␀t␀i␀o␀n␀ ␀o␀f␀ ␀d␀a␀n␀ ␀q␀u␀i␀x␀o␀t␀e␀ ␀w␀h␀i␀c␀h␀ ␀w␀a␀s␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀i␀n␀t␀e␀r␀e␀s␀t␀i␀n␀g␀.␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀t␀h␀e␀n␀ ␀w␀e␀ ␀h␀a␀d␀ ␀a␀ ␀g␀r␀e␀a␀t␀ ␀n␀e␀x␀t␀ ␀d␀a␀y␀ ␀-␀ ␀-␀ ␀l␀e␀i␀s␀u␀r␀e␀l␀y␀ ␀b␀r␀e␀a␀k␀f␀a␀s␀t␀ , ␀ ␀w␀h␀i␀t␀e␀ ␀w␀a␀t␀e␀r␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀r␀a␀f␀t␀i␀n␀g␀ , ␀ ␀v␀i␀s␀i␀t␀ ␀t␀o␀ ␀a␀ ␀l␀o␀c␀a␀l␀ ␀w␀i␀n␀e␀r␀y␀ ␀w␀h␀e␀r␀e␀ ␀w␀e␀ ␀s␀t␀a␀y␀e␀d␀ ␀f␀o␀r␀ ␀a␀n␀ ␀o␀p␀e␀n␀ ␀a␀i␀r␀ ␀b␀l␀u␀e␀ ␀g␀r␀a␀s␀s␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀c␀o␀n␀c␀e␀r␀t␀.␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀v␀e␀r␀y␀ ␀r␀e␀l␀a␀x␀e␀d␀ ␀ & ␀ ␀s␀p␀o␀n␀t␀a␀n␀e␀o␀u␀s␀ ␀d␀a␀y␀ ! ␀ ␀t␀h␀e␀ ␀n␀e␀x␀t␀ ␀d␀a␀y␀ ␀w␀e␀ ␀h␀a␀d␀ ␀t␀h␀e␀ ␀ ␀t␀h␀ ␀o␀f␀ ␀j␀u␀l␀y␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀a␀c␀t␀i␀v␀i␀t␀i␀e␀s␀ , ␀ ␀a␀ ␀l␀o␀n␀g␀ ␀n␀a␀p␀ , ␀ ␀a␀n␀d␀ ␀a␀n␀ ␀a␀m␀a␀z␀i␀n␀g␀ ␀p␀r␀o␀d␀u␀c␀t␀i␀o␀n␀ ␀o␀f␀ ␀w␀e␀r␀e␀ ␀m␀a␀n␀y␀ ␀s␀i␀t␀e␀s␀ ␀l␀e␀f␀t␀ ␀t␀o␀ ␀b␀e␀ ␀s␀e␀e␀n␀ ␀-␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀-␀ ␀l␀a␀v␀a␀ ␀t␀u␀b␀e␀s␀ , ␀ ␀h␀i␀g␀h␀ ␀m␀o␀u␀n␀t␀a␀i␀n␀ ␀d␀e␀s␀e␀r␀t␀ ␀m␀u␀s␀e␀u␀m␀s␀.␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀w␀e␀ ␀d␀o␀ ␀h␀o␀p␀e␀ ␀t␀o␀ ␀f␀o␀r␀t␀ ␀c␀l␀o␀t␀s␀o␀p␀ ␀t␀o␀ ␀s␀e␀e␀ ␀w␀h␀e␀r␀e␀ ␀l␀e␀w␀i␀s␀ ␀ & ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀c␀l␀a␀r␀k␀ ␀ & ␀ ␀t␀h␀e␀i␀r␀ ␀p␀a␀r␀t␀y␀ ␀m␀a␀d␀e␀ ␀a␀ ␀u␀ ␀t␀u␀r␀n␀ , ␀ ␀i␀f␀ ␀y␀o␀u␀ ␀w␀i␀l␀l␀.␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀t␀h␀e␀n␀ ␀w␀e␀ ␀t␀u␀r␀n␀e␀d␀ ␀i␀n␀-␀l␀a␀n␀d␀ ␀ & ␀ ␀h␀e␀a␀d␀e␀d␀ ␀t␀o␀ ␀p␀o␀r␀t␀l␀a␀n␀d␀ ␀w␀h␀e␀r␀e␀ ␀w␀e␀ ␀w␀o␀u␀l␀d␀ ␀s␀p␀e␀n␀d␀ ␀a␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀l␀o␀n␀g␀ ␀f␀e␀s␀t␀i␀v␀e␀ ␀f␀r␀i␀e␀n␀d␀l␀y␀ ␀w␀e␀e␀k␀e␀n␀d␀ ␀w␀i␀t␀h␀ ␀ ␀ ␀o␀f␀ ␀m␀y␀ ␀c␀o␀l␀l␀e␀g␀e␀ ␀b␀u␀d␀d␀i␀e␀s␀ ␀a␀l␀o␀n␀g␀ ␀w␀i␀t␀h␀ ␀s␀o␀m␀e␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀h␀u␀s␀b␀a␀n␀d␀s␀ ␀ & ␀ ␀k␀i␀d␀s␀.␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀a␀g␀a␀i␀n␀ ␀w␀e␀ ␀w␀e␀r␀e␀ ␀s␀t␀a␀y␀i␀n␀g␀ ␀i␀n␀ ␀a␀ ␀f␀a␀n␀t␀a␀s␀t␀i␀c␀ ␀h␀o␀s␀t␀e␀l␀ ␀i␀n␀ ␀d␀o␀w␀n␀t␀o␀w␀n␀ ␀p␀o␀r␀t␀l␀a␀n␀d␀ ␀ & ␀ ␀w␀e␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀w␀a␀l␀k␀e␀d␀ ␀e␀v␀e␀r␀y␀w␀h␀e␀r␀e␀ ␀t␀h␀e␀ ␀e␀n␀t␀i␀r␀e␀ ␀t␀i␀m␀e␀ ␀w␀e␀ ␀w␀e␀r␀e␀ ␀t␀h␀e␀r␀e␀ ␀e␀x␀c␀e␀p␀t␀ ␀f␀o␀r␀ ␀o␀u␀r␀ ␀j␀a␀u␀n␀t␀ ␀t␀o␀ ␀t␀h␀e␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀w␀a␀t␀e␀r␀f␀a␀l␀l␀s␀ ␀ & ␀ ␀t␀h␀e␀ ␀g␀o␀r␀g␀e␀.␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀i␀ ␀g␀u␀e␀s␀s␀ ␀y␀o␀u␀'␀v␀e␀ ␀s␀e␀e␀ ␀'␀v␀e␀o␀m␀e␀ ␀o␀f␀ ␀t␀h␀e␀ ␀p␀h␀o␀t␀o␀s␀ ␀o␀n␀ ␀k␀e␀l␀l␀y␀ ␀'␀s␀ ␀f␀a␀c␀e␀b␀o␀o␀k␀ ␀ & ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀t␀h␀e␀s␀e␀ ␀a␀r␀e␀ ␀s␀o␀m␀e␀ ␀o␀f␀ ␀t␀h␀e␀ ␀s␀t␀o␀r␀i␀e␀s␀ ␀b␀e␀h␀i␀n␀d␀ ␀t␀h␀e␀ ␀p␀i␀c␀t␀u␀r␀e␀s␀.␀ ␀ ␀ ␀ ␀a␀ ␀l␀i␀t␀t␀l␀e␀ ␀w␀h␀i␀l␀e␀ ␀a␀f␀t␀e␀r␀ ␀i␀ ␀g␀o␀t␀ evidence general social survey suggests stress may factor .
stress smoking psychological stress defined `` internal subjective state involving perception threat one 's well-being , '' stressors stimuli provoke psychological stress susceptible individuals .
stressors shown increase tobacco consumption among established smokers , promote adolescent cigarette use , predispose smoking clinic failure , precipitate relapses among successful quitters .
`` , , investigators evaluated variety potential stressors , including anxiety-inducing tasks , family turmoil , job factors , major life events ( e.g. , unemployment , divorce ) .
, lazarus colleagues recently argued daily hassles may represent better conceptualization stress traditional measures major life events .
define hassles `` initiating , frustrating , distressing demands degree characterize everyday transactions environment . ''
hassles appear predict many health outcomes , including psychological distress , somatic symptoms , respiratory illness episodes , arthritis-related disability .
-month follow-up study ex-smokers participated smoking cessation program , frequency severity hassles significant predictors late relapse men , women .
similar evaluation work-site smoking cessation program found mean severity hassles , count , predicted relapse months .
previous studies addressed relationship hassles prevalence smoking community sample .
given role smoking psychological coping behavior , hypothesized persons reporting numerous hassles would likely smoke experiencing fewer stressors .
graph , table , figure , etc removed social support smoking extensive epidemiologic evidence relationship social support diverse health outcomes .
persons high levels social support lower mortality rates , less coronary atherosclerosis , use less health care lower levels support .
social support may exert salutary effects partially fostering changes health-related behaviors , smoking .
indeed , successful quitters higher levels perceived support partners smokers never quit relapse .
someone talk one 's problems predicted cessation another recent study .

therefore hypothesized persons poor social support would likely smoke good support .
buffering stress-support interaction mechanism social support improves health outcomes subject intense debate .
advocates buffering hypothesis argue social support works moderating deleterious effect stressful events serving `` coping assistance . ''
therefore , final hypothesis association social support smoking would stronger among persons experiencing stressors among low-stressor environments .

molecular biology revolutionized study protein structure function .
microbial production enzymes proteins useful amounts become routine recent years , systematic alteration protein sequence site-directed mutagenesis standard tool dissecting chemical mechanism .
recent development new methods ligating peptides , however , promises make chemical synthesis large proteins attractive alternative biosynthesis , particularly construction novel molecules containing non-natural amino acids structural modifications .
attempts synthesize proteins chemically traditionally followed one two basic strategies : stepwise assembly constituent amino acids solid support , convergent coupling peptide segments solution .
although solid-phase peptide synthesis optimized extent proteins amino acids length prepared favorable cases [ l ] , syntheses still represent heroic undertakings number side products accumulate many coupling steps necessary often makes purification final product laborious .
convergent coupling considerable advantage synthesis purification peptide segments residues long relatively straightforward ; since variety unusual amino acids introduced segment , approach also highly adaptable preparation multiple analogs naturally-occurring proteins .
broad implementation fragment condensation strategy limited poor solubility protected peptide segments tendency a-carboxy-activated peptides racemize .
many difficulties circumvented enzymatic coupling procedures .
proteolytic enzymes , particularly serine proteases , used extensively peptide synthesis selectivity , ability function mild reaction conditions , minimal requirements protecting groups [ ] .
although might seem counterintuitive attempt synthesize protein using protease , problems due proteolytic degradation final peptide product catalyst minimized manipulating reaction conditions genetic engineering .
example , replacement active site serine subtilisin cysteine converts protease acyl transferase : modified enzyme readily acylated esters subsequently deacylated amines , greatly diminished amidase activity compared wild-type subtilisin [ ] .
however , substitution sulfur oxygen engenders steric crowding active site , limiting activity enzyme .
wells colleagues [ ] relieved crowding mutating second residue active site.the resulting double mutant , dubbed subtiligase , � eficiently couples unprotected peptides water .
acyl donor typical subtiligase-catalyzed ligation ( fig. )
peptide whose carboxyl terminus esterified glycolate-phenylalanine amide group .
ester efficiently acylates subtiligase enzyme�s preference glycine phenylalanine two positions directly following cleavage� site , pl� p � , respectively .
deacylation enzyme fully deprotected peptide ( protein ) corresponding desired acceptor fragment yields ligated product .

given observation current partition , sorting evaluates quality new clusterings result placing observation existing clusters , quality clustering results creating new cluster covers new observation ; option yields highest quality score ( e.g. , using pu ) selected .
clustering grows incrementally new observations added .
procedure easily incorporated recursive loop builds tree-structured clusterings : given existing hierarchical clustering , observation sorted relative top-level partition ( i.e. , children root ) ; existing child root chosen include observation , observation sorted relative children node , serves root recursive call .
leaf reached , tree extended downward .

[ fisher ; hadzikadic & yun , ; decaestecker ] .

particular , control strategy suffers ordering effects : different orderings observations may yield different clusterings [ fisher , xu & zard , ] .
thus , initial clustering phase , ( possibly offline ) process iterative optimization seeks uncover better clusterings .
seed selection , reordering , reclustering michalski stepp 's [ michalski & stepp , ] cluster/ seeks optimal k-partitioning data .
first step selects k random ` seed ' observations data .
seeds ` attractors ' around k clusters grown remaining data .
since seed selection greatly impact clustering quality , cluster/ selects k new seeds ` centroids ' k initial clusters .
clustering repeated new seeds .
process iterates improvement quality generated clusterings .
ordering effects sorting related effects arise due differing fixed-k seed selections : initial observations ordering establish initial clusters ` attract ' remaining observations .
general , sorting performs better initial observations diverse areas observation-description space , since facilitates establishment initial clusters reflect different areas .

cases , sorting used pu score described previously .

function table outlines reordering procedure .
recursively extracts list observations probable ( i.e. , largest ) cluster least probable , merges ( i.e. , interleaves ) lists , exiting recursive call - - step , element probable cluster placed first , followed element second probable , forth .
whatever measure guides clustering , observations differing clusters judged dissimilar measure .
thus , measure-independent procedure returns measure-dependent dissimilarity ordering placing observations different clusters back-to-back .
iterative redistribution single observations common long-known form iterative optimization moves single observations cluster cluster search better clustering [ duda & hart , ] .
basic strategy used one form another numerous sort-based algorithms well [ fisher et al. , ] .
idea behind iterative redistribution [ biswas , weinberg , yang & koller , ] simple : observations single-level clustering ` removed ' original cluster resorted relative clustering .
cluster contains one observation , cluster ` removed ' single observation resorted .
process continues two consecutive iterations yield clustering .
isodata algorithm [ duda & hart , ] determines target cluster observation , actually change clustering targets observations determined ; point , observations moved targets , thus altering clustering .
limit sequential version , also described duda hart [ ] , moves observation target identified sorting .
strategy conceptually simple , limited ability overcome local maxima - - reclassification particular observation may true direction better clustering , may perceived objective function applied clustering results resorting single observation .
iterative optimization strategy appears novel clustering literature iterative hierarchical redistribution .
strategy rationalized relative single-observation iterative redistribution : even though moving set observations one cluster another may lead better clustering , movement single observation may initially reduce clustering quality , thus preventing eventual discovery better clustering .
response , hierarchical redistribution considers movement observation sets , represented existing clusters hierarchical clustering .

major religions trace history founder-buddha , moses , jesus , muhammad .
hinduism exception .
one person said founder hinduism , began develop years ago aryan tribes invaded subcontinent brought sacred writings known vedas .
since hinduism passed number stages .
original vedic period hinduism emerged worldly optimistic faith , faithful worshiped various gods nature , god fire , god sky , god storms war .
next period-the brahmanic-the role priests greatly expanded ; developed complex rituals daily life well life 's milestones .
also period basic concepts caste , reincarnation , karma appeared .
beliefs found vedic treatises , dated early th th centuries b. c. known upanishads -- epitome early hindu philosophy .
gradually period , hindus developed belief many gods worshiped really many `` faces '' one ultimate god ( brahman ) also atman ( soul ) living things .
final stage philosophical hinduism explored ways atman could escape cycle births , deaths , rebirths achieve moksha , liberation , brahman .
hinduism acknowledges multitude gods -- say million- one distinguish folk religion indian masses hinduism educated sophisticated elite .
latter see brahman one god takes form gods .
brahman 's chief attributes seen trinity brahma creator , vishnu preserver , siva destroyer .
practice hindus tend ignore brahma direct devotion vishnu siva along personal village gods .
sects agree many paths lead moksha .
vishnu believed appeared nine incarnations date , including popular rama krishna ; tenth incarnation expected , ooo years .
vishnu appears krishna bhagavad gita ( `` song lord '' ) , hinduism 's famous epic poem .
popular hinduism , practiced india 's , villages , seems polytheistic tolerant .
devotees often treat images gods homes temples would person : offer food drink , clothe , bathe , tuck bed night .

billion people identify christians ; group diverse bodies southern baptists , presbyterians , amish , roman catholics , pentecostals , jehovah 's witnesses , lutherans , greek orthodox , mormons , anglicans , seventh-day adventists , copts , quakers , methodists , .

people experience misery frustration seek pleasure achievement world maya , fail find fully real life god .

hindus , doctrine karma may stem impulse improve condition poor sickly .
following reasoning , one would n't envy healthy , powerful , fortunate since enjoying fruits good accomplished previous lives .
however , sophisticated hindus recognize since forms life atman , sacred deserve cared matter present condition .
besides reincarnation karma , caste system central hindu beliefs customs .
aryan invaders conquered indigenous people , created four castes divided three higher castes , assigning conquered people lowest caste .
four castes since divided approximately subcastes .
hindus may change caste lifetimes , may aspire higher caste next incarnation .
caste membership determines many aspects daily life : choice occupation , social status , dress , religious practices , even eating habits .
hindu expected carry dharma , duties , par- ticular caste subcaste .
top system brahmin caste , priests intellectuals .
come kshatriyas ruling warrior caste .
third , vaisyas engage business professions .
lowest caste one includes three fourths indian population sudras , laborers farmers .
completely outside caste system untouchables , destined carry menial tasks .
gandhi called outcastes harijans , children god , urged compassion toward .
constitution indian state declares : `` untouchability abolished , practice form abolished . ''
official legal position little effect life untouchables thousands indian villages .
nevertheless , untouchables attend college aspire better jobs despite social discrimination .

course , people marry within caste .

aristotle two forms metaphysical predication : a. said-of relation b. present-in relation amounts two metaphysically distinct manners properties able predicated subjects .

two relations combine four possible ways , yeilding four types metaphysical entities .
[ ] a. substantial individuals .
substantial individuals neither present-in said-of anything .
examples socrates , individual man , phar lap , individual horse .
substantial individual , socrates , predicated , either present-in said-of relation , subject .
, nothing subject substantial individual .
b. substantial kinds .
substantial kinds said-of something , present-in anything .
examples species genera ; man , horse , animal .
substantial kind , horse , predicated said-of relation subject , phar lap , predicated present-in relation subject .
c. non-substantial individuals .
non-substantial individuals present-in something , said-of anything .
examples midnight black pallor .
non-substantial individual , shade color midnight black , may predicated present-in relation subject , phar lap , yet predicated said-of relation subject .
d. non-substantial kinds .
non-substantial kinds present-in said-of something .
examples color .

criteria primary substances aristotle declares four types metaphysical entities identified substantial individuals ( individual man , say , socrates individual horse , say , phar lap ) ontologically basic play role primary substances .
two criteria employed aristotle delineating general patterns ontological priority dependency identifying substantial individuals primary substances .
one criterion predicative , existential .
a. predicative : neither said-of present-in subject , things predicated .
b. existential : `` exist , would impossible anything exist . ''
section examines arguments aristotle attempts use criteria establish ontological primacy primary substances .
.
predicative criterion predicative criterion consists two parts : ( ) neither said-of present-in subject , ( b ) things predicated .
first part unequivocally picks substantial individuals primary substances .
aristotle states : substance -- called substance strictly , primarily , -- neither said subject subject , e.g .
individual man individual horse .
[ ] clearly identifies substantial individuals primary substances , criterion substantial individuals simply first part criterion primary substances .
socrates phar lap , examples substantial individuals , primary substances .

aristotle states : primary substances subjects things things predicated , called substances .
[ ] elsewhere declares : primary substances subjects everything else called substances strictly .
[ ] amounts three metaphysical entities identified ( , substantial kinds , non-substantial individuals , non-substantial kinds ) predicated , either said-of present-in relation , substantial individuals primary substances .
primary substance therefore subject every instance predication .

organism arnold parker study single- celled , microscopic animal belonging foraminiferida , order hard-shelled , planktonic marine protozoans .
often shortened `` forams , '' name comes latin word foramen , `` opening . ''
organisms likened amoebas wearing shells , perforations protoplasm extends .
foram shell shapes range plain bizarre .
tropical subtropical seas around globe abound forams , divided two general types : free- floating , planktonic form uniformly small ( usually less oth inch long ) benthic bottom-dwelling variety typically much larger .
latter type perhaps best remembered earth-science students spelunkers commonly find fossils imbedded cave walls .
ancient egyptians used limestone blocks containing large , extinct nummulites build tops giza pyramids .
's planktonic variety chiefly interests parker arnold .
unlike oversized cousins , free-swimming forams found almost everywhere oceans .
fossilized skeletons , fact , among first biological material recovered deep ocean bottoms scientists s. nearly century , geologists used tiny fossils help establish age sediments gain insight prehistoric climates .
since early , though , scientists begun fully appreciate fossil forams ' potential tool use evolutionary studies host earth sciences well .

revelation flies face latter-day rethinking darwinian evolution , past years tended gravitate toward new theory called punctuated equilibrium .
first postulated early paleontologists niles eldredge stephen j. gould , idea refutes gradualism 's central premise great amounts time necessary create new species .
punctuated equilibrium holds new species may arise fairly quickly ( thousands instead millions years ) small animal populations somehow become isolated .
intermediate stages fleeting become fixed fossil record-thus conspicuous gaps so-called missing links .
( darwin blamed `` imperfection geological record '' gaps fossil record . )

?
general divide reproduction two categories : asexual sexual .
asexual reproduction oldest simplest form reproduction , one parent 's body divides breaks apart way produce offspring genetically identical parent .
asexual reproduction methods include fission , parent 's body splits two more-or-less equal parts ; budding , small part parent 's body becomes differentiated separates rest ; fragmentation , parent 's body breaks many pieces piece develops offspring ( starfish regrow entire body single broken-off arm way ) .

albert eschenmoser federal institute technology zurich , one deepest-thinking chemists time , focused sugars .
nucleic acids , including dna rna , information carriers life .
two strands dna , marvelous biopolymer , chain phosphates ( po ) alternating sugar rings made carbons single oxygen .
coming rings flat platelet-like molecular units , `` bases '' letters genetic alphabet : adenine ( ) , guanine ( g ) , cytosine ( c ) , thymine ( , dna ) uracil ( u , rna ) .

bionic sounds cochlear transplant research around since s. essence , cochlear implants provide electrical stimulation auditory nerve .

today , addition numerous research institutions developing bionic hearing devices , several companies already selling commercial products .
although differences product , work essentially way .
even profoundly deaf , least one-third , fibers stimulate auditory nerve survive responsive , capable transmitting brain 's hearing center .
signals center triggered cochlear implant .
unlike hearing aids , amplify sound , cochlear implantor bionic eartrades electricity .
surgeons implant device mastoid bone inner ear incision behind ear .

signal fed transmitter coil implanted receiver .

artificial cochleas benefit deaf people .
adults never heard suitable candidates , doctors say , auditory part brains respond stimuli .
entirely different approach , jonathan spindel , biomedical engineer assistant professor university virginia 's department otolaryngology , developing implanted `` invisible '' magnetic hearing aid. ``
tests date shown signals produced magnetic hearing device nearly natural acoustic sound , '' says spindel .
approach , device captures sounds miniature microphone implanted ear .
passing small processing unit electromagnetic coil , also implanted , amplified vibrations would sent inner ear via tiny magnet attached inner ear 's round window , thin membrane one end cochlea .
magnet , large pencil point , would send vibrations cochlea , stimulating thousands hair cells used normal hearing .
spindel says advantage approach device n't obstruct normal hearing process. ``
establishing second independent input pathway inner ear opens possibility using normal acoustic pathway round window electromagnet simultaneously establish constructive destructive sound patterns inner ear. ``
also , since new hearing device uses magnetic rather acoustic vibrations , feedbacka common problem conventional hearing aidsis eliminated .

copyright ?

families entering employment , earnings , caseloads .

however , spite dramatic results , still individuals working entry-level jobs , incomes low erratic raise families poverty .

encourage states take critical next steps ensure families get essential supports need get job , succeed work , move poverty .

u.s. mint : collectors ' corner collecting u.s. coins united states coins made four mint facilities : philadelphia , denver , san francisco , west point , ny .
one easy way start collection circulating coins use daily - pennies , nickels , dimes , quarters dollars .
addition , u.s. mint also issues annual proof uncirculated sets , national medals , commemorative coins .
